english: Comp. dmag-krims (dmag khrims) 'martial law' Cs. -- *mag-ṭúg* (*mag thug*) W. war, contention, contest. -- dmag-mgó (dmag mgo) Ma. vanguard, front or first line of the army. -- dmag-sgár (dmag sgar) encampment, ͵dègs-pa ('degs pa) to pitch a camp. -- dmag-bsgrig (dmag bsgrig) troops drawn up, battle-array Sch -- dmag-čás (dmag chas) requisites for war, military stores, ammunition Pth. -- *mag-táb* (*mag thab*) C., W. war. -- dmag-nór (dmag nor) v. above sub no. 2 mag-nór (mag nor). -- dmag-sná (dmag sna) = dmag-mgó (dmag mgo) Ma. -- dmag-dpúṅ (dmag dpung) army. -- dmag-dpón (dmag dpon) commander, general. -- dmag-bráṅ (dmag brang) = dmag-sgár (dmag sgar). -- dmag - mi (dmag mi) warrior, soldier. -- dmág-mo (dmag mo) = dmag (dmag), dmág-mo če bskúr-ba (dmag mo che bskur ba) Pth. to send out a great army. -- dmag-tsôgs (dmag tshogs) = dmag-dpúṅ (dmag dpung). -- dmag-líṅs (dmag lings) v. above.

the common people, populace, multitude, vulgar; dmáṅs-kyi stón-mo (dmangs kyi ston mo) a banquet for all Mil.; dmáṅs pál-pa (dmangs phal pa) the vulgar, the common people; one of the common people; dmáṅs-rigs (dmangs rigs) id.; used also as an abusive word: mean fellow; when referred to Indian matters = Ssk. शूद्र, the caste of craftsmen, not so low as gdól-ba (gdol ba).
Sch. invective, abuse, (does not suit to S.g. 21).

(cf. dmá-ba (dma' ba)) 1. low, v. mtó-ba (mtho ba); gen. fig., in reference to quantity, little, dman lhag log (dman lhag log) either too little, or too much, or badly constituted, e.g. gall, and other humours of the human body Med.; bsód-nams dmán-pa (bsod nams dman pa) having little merit, blo dmán-pa (blo dman pa) having little sense Glr.; with skye-ba (skye ba) v. skye-ba (skye ba) II.; in reference to quality: indifferent, inferior Ssk. हीन, rím-pas dáṅ-po mčog yin p̓yí-ma dman (rim pas dang po mchog yin phyi ma dman) in the order (of enumeration) the first is always better, the next following inferior S.g.; *men-sár* (*men sar*) maiden, girl, virgin C. (cf. skye-dmán (skye dman)); depressed in spirits Wdn.; poor, pitiable, ri-dwágs dmán-ma (ri dwags dman ma) the poor deer Mil.; dmán-sa (dman sa) or dman-ča (dman cha), ͵dzín-pa ('dzin pa) to choose the low, humble part, to be humble, to humble one's self, frq.; dmán-sa zuṅ daṅ mtó-sar sleb (dman sa zung dang mtho sar sleb) Mil. choose what is low, and you will obtain what is high. -- 2. dman (dman) for skye-dmán (skye dman) woman, opp. to p̓o (pho) Mil. -- 3. in Mil. sometimes also for má-mo (ma mo), srín-mo (srin mo).

profit, gain, good success, dmar čuṅ (dmar chung) a small profit Mil; dmár-po (dmar po) adj., ṭugs-dám dmár-po byúṅ-ṅam (thugs dam dmar po byung ngam) did it go on well with your meditation? Mil., dmar-Krid (dmar khrid) Cs. 'practical instruction', e.g. in the healing art; acc. to my authorities it signifies the last 'finishing' instruction, in religion Mil., in medical science Med.

fem. dmár-mo (dmar mo) (seldom), dmár-ba (dmar ba), adj. 1. red, frq., mdog-dmár-po (mdog dmar po) one red-coloured (lit. red as to colour) Dom.; dmár-baí spyan (dmar ba'i spyan) red eyes Glr.; sna dmár-ba (sna dmar ba) having a red trunk or proboscis Glr.; dmár-ba (dmar ba), also redness and to be red. -- 2. v. dmar (dmar). -- Comp. dmar-skyá (dmar skya) pale red. -- dmar-Krá (dmar khra) Lt., red-spotted. -- dmar-Krid (dmar khrid) v. dmar (dmar). -- *mar-żéṅ* (*mar zheng*) raw meat W. -- dmar-ljáṅ (dmar ljang) greenish red Mil. -- *már-tag č̣ód* (*mar thag chod*) W. the red of evening has vanished from the mountains. -- dmar-táb? (dmar thab ?) -- dmar-tór (dmar thor) v. tór-pa (thor pa). -- dmar-mdáṅs (dmar mdangs) Sch. 1. bright red (?) 2. ruddy complexion. -- dmar-͵dón (dmar 'don) Lt. medicinal herb; in Lh. = bya-po-tsi-tsi (bya po tsi tsi). -- dmar-nág (dmar nag), skud-pa dmar-nág rnyis (skud pa dmar nag rnyis) two threads, one black, the other red, used in magic. -- dmar-smyúg (dmar smyug) blackish red. -- mar-zaṅ-záṅ (mar zang zang) scarlet-red. -- dmar-yól (dmar yol) red china-ware (? opp. to dkar-yól (dkar yol)) Med. -- dmar-bšál (dmar bshal) Sch. dysentery, bloody flux. -- dmar-sér (dmar ser) (-po) (- po) reddish yellow, honey-coloured Glr.

1. vb. (analogous to sgom-pa (sgom pa)), to fancy, to imagine Tar. 73, 5. prob.; to think, to construe in one's mind, dmígs-te (dmigs te) Glr. or vulgo dmígs-la (dmigs la) in imagination, e.g. to do a thing in one's mind, which at the time one is not able to perform in reality; this according to a Buddhist's belief is permitted in various cases (e.g. *sém-mi míg-la púl-če* (*sem mi mig la pul che*) W., to bring an offering in mind, in imagination); it is attended with the same beneficial effects, as
